perturbation.original_text: Before you open the Franchise and during any Term of this Agreement, you must maintain in force, under policies of insurance written on an occurrence basis issued by carriers with an A.M. Best rating of A-VIII or better approved by us, and in such amounts as we may determine from time to time: (1) comprehensive public, professional, product, medical malpractice and motor vehicle liability insurance against claims for bodily and personal injury, death and property damage caused by or occurring in conjunction with the operation of the Franchise or otherwise in conjunction with your conduct of the Franchise Business pursuant to this Agreement, under one or more policies of insurance containing minimum liability coverage amounts as set forth in the Operations Manual; (2) general casualty insurance, including theft, cash theft, fire and extended coverage, vandalism and malicious mischief insurance, for the replacement value of the Franchise and its contents, and any other assets of the Franchise; (3) worker's compensation and employer's liability insurance as required by law, with limits equal to or in excess of those required by statute; (4) business interruption insurance for a period adequate to reestablish normal business operations, but in any event not less than six (6) months; (5) any other insurance required by applicable law, rule, regulation, ordinance or licensing requirements; and (6) umbrella liability coverage with limits of not less than $1,000,000/$3,000,000 or such other amounts that we may establish in the Operations Manual.
perturbation.changed_text: Before you open the Franchise and during any Term of this Agreement, you must maintain in force, under policies of insurance issued by carriers approved by us, and in such amounts as we may determine from time to time: (1) public liability insurance against claims for bodily and personal injury, death and property damage caused by or occurring in conjunction with the operation of the Franchise or otherwise in conjunction with your conduct of the Franchise Business pursuant to this Agreement; (2) general casualty insurance, including theft, fire, vandalism and malicious mischief insurance, for the replacement value of the Franchise and its contents, and any other assets of the Franchise; (3) worker's compensation and employer's liability insurance as required by law; (4) business interruption insurance; (5) any other insurance required by applicable law, rule, regulation, ordinance or licensing requirements.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specificity of 'occurrence basis,' the A.M. Best rating requirement, the 'comprehensive' and 'professional/product/medical malpractice/motor vehicle' qualifications from public liability insurance, the 'limits equal to or in excess of those required by statute' from worker's compensation, the 'adequate to reestablish normal business operations, but in any event not less than six (6) months' term from business interruption insurance, and finally removing the umbrella liability insurance, this change weakens the franchisee's insurance requirements. Specifically, by removing a coverage threshold, we introduce legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Insurance requirements for businesses in Arizona
perturbation.law_explanation: This section mandates workers' compensation insurance, but by removing the requirement that the policy limits equal or exceed the statutory requirements, the contract allows for policies that do not fully comply with the law. Additionally, by removing umbrella liability coverage (potentially a requirement dependent on the specific business), and removing that the insurance be written on an occurrence basis, this could lead to the franchisee carrying inadequate or non-compliant insurance.

ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 23 Next Section Printable Version 23-902 - Employers subject to chapter; exceptions 23-902. Employers subject to chapter; exceptions A. Employers subject to this chapter are the state, each county, city, town, municipal corporation and school district and every person who employs any workers or operatives regularly employed in the same business or establishment under contract of hire, including covered employees pursuant to a professional employer agreement, except domestic servants. Exempted employers of domestic servants may come under this chapter by complying with its provisions and the rules of the commission. For the purposes of this subsection, "regularly employed" includes all employments, whether continuous throughout the year, or for only a portion of the year, in the usual trade, business, profession or occupation of an employer. B. When an employer procures work to be done for the employer by a contractor over whose work the employer retains supervision or control, and the work is a part or process in the trade or business of the employer, then the contractors and the contractor's employees, and any subcontractor and the subcontractor's employees, are, within the meaning of this section, employees of the original employer. For the purposes of this subsection, "part or process in the trade or business of the employer" means a particular work activity that in the context of an ongoing and integral business process is regular, ordinary or routine in the operation of the business or is routinely done through the business' own employees. C. A person engaged in work for a business, and who while so engaged is independent of that business in the execution of the work and not subject to the rule or control of the business for which the work is done, but is engaged only in the performance of a definite job or piece of work, and is subordinate to that business only in effecting a result in accordance with that business design, is an independent contractor. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific insurance coverage levels and types (A.M. Best rating, occurrence basis, specific liability coverages, minimum term for business interruption, umbrella liability). The changed text weakens these requirements by omitting these specifics, increasing the risk of inadequate coverage and potential non-compliance. The Law Explanation highlights that removing the 'limits equal to or in excess of those required by statute' for worker's compensation allows for policies that do not fully comply with the law. Though the snippets don't explicitly detail specific insurance requirements, the Perturbation Explanation clearly states that the omission of these specifics weakens the franchisee's insurance requirements, potentially leading to legal risk. Therefore, by removing legally relevant obligations, a legal contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: You agree to maintain, at your own expense, the Joint Software and accounting software, to act as a bookkeeping, accounting, and record keeping system for the Franchise. The Joint Software includes the capability of being polled by our central computer system, which you agree to permit. With respect to the operation and financial condition of the Franchise, we will pull from the Joint Software (if available), or require you to provide from your accounting software in a form we designate, or in accordance with General Acceptably Accounting Principles ("GAAP"), as the case may be, the following: (1) by Tuesday of each week, an electronic report of the Franchise's gross revenues for the preceding week ending on, and including, Sunday, and any other data, information, and supporting records that we may require; (2) by the thirtieth (30 t h) day of each month, a profit and loss statement for the preceding calendar month, and a year-to-date profit and loss statement and balance sheet; (3) within ninety (90) days after the end of your fiscal year, a fiscal year-end balance sheet, and an annual profit and loss statement for that fiscal year, reflecting all year- end adjustments; and (4) such other reports as we require from time to time (collectively, the "Reports"). You agree to input all Franchise transactions into the Joint Software and your accounting software in a timely manner to ensure that all Reports are accurate. If it is determined that any information was omitted from the Joint Software or your accounting software was input inaccurately, or you have failed to provide us any required Reports, we may charge a non-refundable accounting fee of One Hundred and No/100 Dollars ($100.00), payable in a lump sum by the fifth (5th) day of the month following the month during which the inaccurate report was submitted or for any late Reports. You agree to maintain and furnish upon our request complete copies of federal and state income tax returns you file with the Internal Revenue Service and state tax departments, reflecting revenues and income of the Franchise or the corporation, partnership, or limited liability company that holds the Franchise.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree to maintain, at your own expense, the Joint Software and accounting software, to act as a bookkeeping, accounting, and record keeping system for the Franchise. The Joint Software includes the capability of being polled by our central computer system, which you agree to permit. With respect to the operation and financial condition of the Franchise, we will pull from the Joint Software (if available), or require you to provide from your accounting software in a form we designate, the following: (1) by Tuesday of each week, an electronic report of the Franchise's gross revenues for the preceding week ending on, and including, Sunday, and any other data, information, and supporting records that we may require; (2) by the thirtieth (30 t h) day of each month, a profit and loss statement for the preceding calendar month, and a year-to-date profit and loss statement and balance sheet; (3) within ninety (90) days after the end of your fiscal year, a fiscal year-end balance sheet, and an annual profit and loss statement for that fiscal year, reflecting all year- end adjustments; and (4) such other reports as we require from time to time (collectively, the "Reports"). You agree to input all Franchise transactions into the Joint Software and your accounting software in a timely manner to ensure that all Reports are accurate. If it is determined that any information was omitted from the Joint Software or your accounting software was input inaccurately, or you have failed to provide us any required Reports, we may charge a non-refundable accounting fee of One Hundred and No/100 Dollars ($100.00), payable in a lump sum by the fifth (5th) day of the month following the month during which the inaccurate report was submitted or for any late Reports.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "You agree to maintain and furnish upon our request complete copies of federal and state income tax returns you file with the Internal Revenue Service and state tax departments, reflecting revenues and income of the Franchise or the corporation, partnership, or limited liability company that holds the Franchise.", we remove an element of financial transparency. Further, by removing the need for GAAP accounting, we allow for the possibility that financial statements can be prepared in ways that may not be appropriate and could be deemed fraudulent if done improperly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Revised Statutes regarding fraudulent schemes and practices
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing non-GAAP accounting and removing the requirement for the franchisee to provide tax returns, the contract increases the risk that the franchisee could engage in fraudulent financial reporting. This section outlines that "The act, use or employment by any person of any deception, deceptive act or practice, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation, or concealment, suppression or omission of any material fact with intent that others rely upon such concealment, suppression or omission, in connection with the sale or advertisement of any merchandise whether or not any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby, is declared unlawful." This can extend to operating a business based on non-GAAP financials, since there are franchise fees directly tied to revenue. The omission of the requirement to supply tax returns makes it more difficult to verify if there are deceptive acts.

ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 44 Next Section Printable Version 44-1220 - Fraudulent insurance claim; classification 44-1220 . Fraudulent insurance claim; classification A person who presents a false or fraudulent claim or proof in support of such claim, upon a contract of insurance for payment of any loss, or who prepares, makes or subscribes to an account, certificate of survey, affidavit or proof of loss, or other book, paper or writing, with intent to present or use it or allow it to be presented or used in support of such claim, is guilty of a class 5 felony. perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for the franchisee to provide federal and state income tax returns, reflecting revenues and income of the Franchise. The law explanation states that this omission increases the risk that the franchisee could engage in fraudulent financial reporting and makes it more difficult to verify deceptive acts. While the scraped law snippet mentions "Fraudulent insurance claim; classification", focusing on false claims related to insurance, the Law explanation provides a connection from the omitted information to deceptive practices. The original contract ensures transparency by allowing franchisor access to tax returns, and the removal of this condition facilitates financial misrepresentation and potential fraud, especially given that franchise fees are revenue based.

perturbation.original_text: All advertising you employ must be completely factual, in good taste (in our judgment), and conform to the highest standards of ethical advertising and all legal requirements. You agree that in all dealings with us and any of our affiliates, other franchisees, your customers, your suppliers, and public officials, you will adhere to the highest standards of honesty, integrity, fair dealing and ethical conduct. You further agree to refrain from any business or advertising practice that may be harmful to the business of the Company, the Franchise, and/or the goodwill associated with the Marks and other The Joint Corp. franchises.
perturbation.changed_text: All advertising you employ must be completely factual and conform to all legal requirements. You agree that in all dealings with us and any of our affiliates, other franchisees, your customers, your suppliers, and public officials, you will adhere to the standards of honesty and integrity. You further agree to refrain from any business or advertising practice that may be harmful to the business of the Company, the Franchise, and/or the goodwill associated with the Marks and other The Joint Corp. franchises.
perturbation.explanation: Removing "in good taste (in our judgment)" removes some of the franchisor's ability to ensure compliance. Removing "highest standards of ethical advertising" and replacing "highest standards of honesty, integrity, fair dealing and ethical conduct" with "standards of honesty and integrity" lowers the ethical bar, creating more opportunities for potentially deceptive or misleading advertising that, while technically legal, might be considered unethical or unfair and could impact consumers.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Consumer Fraud Act - Deceptive Practices
perturbation.law_explanation: By lowering the ethical advertising standards, the franchise contract becomes more likely to permit advertising that, while technically factual, could be considered misleading or deceptive, violating the Arizona Consumer Fraud Act. This section prohibits false or misleading advertising, and the reduced ethical standard increases the risk of non-compliance, since even technically 'factual' statements can still be deceptive in their overall presentation.

ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 44 Next Section Printable Version 44-1522 - Unlawful practices; intended interpretation of provisions 44-1522 . Unlawful practices; intended interpretation of provisions A. The act, use or employment by any person of any deception, deceptive or unfair act or practice, fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation, or concealment, suppression or omission of any material fact with intent that others rely on such concealment, suppression or omission, in connection with the sale or advertisement of any merchandise whether or not any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby, is declared to be an unlawful practice. B. The violation of chapter 9, article 16 or chapter 19, article 1 of this title is declared to be an unlawful practice and subject to enforcement under this article. C. It is the intent of the legislature, in construing subsection A, that the courts may use as a guide interpretations given by the federal trade commission and the federal courts to 15 United States Code sections 45, 52 and 55(a)(1). perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for advertising to be 'in good taste (in our judgment)' and lowers the ethical standards for franchisee conduct. Arizona Revised Statutes § 44-1522(A) declares unlawful any 'deception, deceptive or unfair act or practice... misrepresentation... in connection with the sale or advertisement of any merchandise.' By removing the 'good taste' and 'highest standards of ethical advertising' requirements, the modified contract increases the risk that advertising, while technically factual, could still be deceptive or misleading, thus contradicting the statute's intent to prevent deceptive advertising practices.
